1.1: 12-14. This description of treachery does not deny, but aggravates, the injury from enemies.
1.7:
abideth--

19. God hears the wicked in wrath. or, "sitteth." enthroned as a sovereign. Prosperity hardens them ( Psa 73:5 ).
1.19: 5-6. A vivid picture of the conduct of malicious enemies.
1.31:
1. my soul--

or self, or life, which is threatened. ( Psa 17:8 ; 36:7 ). literally, "mischiefs" ( Psa 52:2 ; 55:10 ).

2.17: 3-5. describe the wicked generally, who sin naturally, easily, malignantly, and stubbornly.

3.12:
belch out--

6-7. They are as ravening dogs seeking prey, and as such, that is, slanders, their impudent barkings.
3.18:
12. let them even be ... taken in their pride--

while evincing it--that is, to be punished for their lies, &c.
3.24:
sing aloud ... in the morning--

16-17. Contrast the lot of God's servant, who employs his time in God's praise. when they retire famishing and disappointed, or it may denote delightful diligence in praise, as in Psa 30:5 .

4.3: 4-5. Yet to God's banner they will rally, and pray that, led and sustained by His power (right hand, Psa 17:7 ; 20:6 ), they may be safe.

5.2:
6-10. God hath spoken in--

or, "by." ( Psa 89:35 ; Am 4:2 ), on the pledge of His attributes ( Psa 22:3 ; 30:4 ). Taking courage from God's promise to give them possession ( Ex 23:31 ; De 11:24 ) (and perhaps renewed to him by special revelation), with triumphant joy he describes the conquest as already made. as widely separated points, and--
5.8:
1-3. From the end--

that is, places remote from the sanctuary ( De 28:64 ).
5.14:
6-7. the king--

himself and his royal line ending in Christ. Mercy and truth personified, as in Psa 40:11 ; 57:3 .
5.20:
be greatly moved--

2. The titles applied to God often occur ( Psa 9:9 ; 18:2 ). ( Psa 10:6 ). No injury shall be permanent, though devised by enemies.
5.26:
7. rock of my strength--

or strongest support ( Psa 7:10 ; 61:3 ).

6.1:
12. for thou renderest--

literally, "that Thou renderest," &c., connected with "I heard this," as the phrase--"that power," &c. [Psa 62:11]--teaching that by His power He can show both mercy and justice.
6.7:
4. Thus--

literally, "Truly." praise Thee ( Psa 34:1 ). in worship (compare Psa 28:2 ). in praise of Thy perfections.
6.13:
9-10. those ... to destroy it--

or literally, "to ruin," or, "for ruin"; that is, such as seek to injure me (are) for ruin, appointed to it (compare Psa 35:8 ). into the grave, or, to death; as their bodies are represented as a portion for--

7.5:
2. insurrection--

literally, "uproar," noisy assaults, as well as their secret counsels.
7.11: 7. The contrast is heightened by representing God as using weapons like theirs.
7.17:
1. Praise waiteth for thee--

literally, "To Thee silence praise," or (compare Psa 62:1 ), To Thee silence is praise--that is, Praise is waiting as a servant; it is due to Thee. So the last clause expresses the duty of paying vows. These two parts of acceptable worship, mentioned in Psa 50:14 , are rendered in Zion, where God chiefly displays His mercy and receives homage.
7.23: 6-13. God's great power and goodness are the grounds of this confidence. These are illustrated in His control of the mightiest agencies of nature and nations affecting men with awe and dread ( Psa 26:7 ; 98:1 , &c.), and in His fertilizing showers, causing the earth to produce abundantly for man and beast.

8.3: 6-13. God's great power and goodness are the grounds of this confidence. These are illustrated in His control of the mightiest agencies of nature and nations affecting men with awe and dread ( Psa 26:7 ; 98:1 , &c.), and in His fertilizing showers, causing the earth to produce abundantly for man and beast.
8.9:
2. his name--

as in Psa 29:2 . literally, "place honor, His praise," or, "as to His praise"; that is, let His praise be such as will glorify Him, or, be honorable to Him.
8.15:
7. behold the nations--

watch their conduct.
8.21: 10-12. Out of severe trials, God had brought them to safety (compare Is 48:10 ; 1Pe 1:7 ).

9.4: 16-20. With these he unites his public thanks, inviting those who fear God ( Psa 60:4 ; 61:5 , His true worshippers) to hear. He vindicates his sincerity, inasmuch as God would not hear hypocrites, but had heard him.
9.10:
1. cause his face to shine--

show us favor ( Nu 6:24 , 25 ; Psa 31:16 ).
9.16: 6-7. The blessings of a fruitful harvest are mentioned as types of greater and spiritual blessings, under which all nations shall fear and love God.

10.6:
thou wentest--

7-8. (Compare Ex 19:16-18 ). in the pillar of fire. literally, "in Thy tread," Thy majestic movement.
10.12: 13. Some translate this, "When ye shall lie between the borders, ye shall," &c., comparing the peaceful rest in the borders or limits of the promised land to the proverbial beauty of a gentle dove. Others understand by the word rendered "pots," the smoked sides of caves, in which the Israelites took refuge from enemies in the times of the judges; or, taking the whole figuratively, the rows of stones on which cooking vessels were hung; and thus that a contrast is drawn between their former low and afflicted state and their succeeding prosperity. In either case, a state of quiet and peace is described by a beautiful figure.
10.18:
18. From the scene of conquest He ascends to His throne, leading--

or, "many captives captive" ( Jud 5:12 ). accepting their homage, even when forced, as that of rebels. or literally, "to dwell, O Lord God" (compare Psa 68:16 )--that is, to make this hill, His people or Church, His dwelling. This Psalm typifies the conquests of the Church under her divine leader, Christ. He, indeed, "who was with the Church in the wilderness" ( Ac 7:38 ) is the Lord, described in this ideal ascension. Hence Paul ( Ep 4:8 ) applies this language to describe His real ascension, when, having conquered sin, death, and hell, the Lord of glory triumphantly entered heaven, attended by throngs of adoring angels, to sit on the throne and wield the scepter of an eternal dominion. The phrase "received gifts for (or literally, among) men" is by Paul, "gave gifts to men." Both describe the acts of a conqueror, who receives and distributes spoils. The Psalmist uses "receiving" as evincing the success, Paul "gave" as the act, of the conqueror, who, having subdued his enemies, proceeds to reward his friends. The special application of the passage by Paul was in proof of Christ's exaltation. What the Old Testament represents of His descending and ascending corresponds with His history. He who descended is the same who has ascended. As then ascension was an element of His triumph, so is it now; and He, who, in His humiliation, must be recognized as our vicarious sacrifice and the High Priest of our profession, must also be adored as Head of His Church and author of all her spiritual benefits.
10.30: 28-29. Thanks for the past, and confident prayer for the future victories of Zion are mingled in a song of praise.

11.1:
32-36. To Him who is presented as riding in triumph through His ancient heavens and proclaiming His presence--

to Him who, in nature, and still more in the wonders of His spiritual government, out of His holy place ( Psa 43:3 ), is terrible, who rules His Church, and, by His Church, rules the world in righteousness--let all nations and kingdoms give honor and power and dominion evermore.
11.7:
come in unto my soul--

1-2. (Compare Psa 40:2 ). literally, "come even to my soul," endanger my life by drowning ( Jon 2:5 ).
11.13: 7-12. This plea contemplates his relation to God as a sufferer in His cause. Reproach, domestic estrangement ( Mr 3:21 ; Joh 7:5 ), exhaustion in God's service ( Joh 2:17 ), revilings and taunts of base men were the sufferings.

12.2: 7-12. This plea contemplates his relation to God as a sufferer in His cause. Reproach, domestic estrangement ( Mr 3:21 ; Joh 7:5 ), exhaustion in God's service ( Joh 2:17 ), revilings and taunts of base men were the sufferings.

13.1: 16-18. These earnest terms are often used, and the address to God, as indifferent or averse, is found in Psa 3:7 ; 22:24 ; 27:9 , &c.
13.7:
welfare--

22-23. With unimportant verbal changes, this language is used by Paul to describe the rejection of the Jews who refused to receive the Saviour ( Ro 11:9 , 10 ). The purport of the figures used is that blessings shall become curses, the "table" of joy (as one of food) a "snare," their literally, "peaceful condition," or security, a "trap." Darkened eyes and failing strength complete the picture of the ruin falling on them under the invoked retribution.
13.13:
27-28. iniquity--

or, "punishment of iniquity" ( Psa 40:12 ). partake of its benefits.
13.19:
your heart, &c.--

32-33. Others shall rejoice. "Humble" and poor, as in Psa 69:29 . address to such (compare Psa 22:26 ).

14.20:
my praise ... of thee--

6-9. His history from early infancy illustrated God's care, and his wonderful deliverances were at once occasions of praise and ground of confidence for the future. literally, "in" or "by Thee" ( Psa 22:25 ).
14.26: 14-16. The ruin of his enemies, as illustrating God's faithfulness, is his deliverance, and a reason for future confidence.
14.32:
taught me, &c.--

17-21. Past experience again encourages. by providential dealings.

15.5:
will ... praise--

22-24. To the occasion of praise he now adds the promise to render it. literally, "will thank." as to Thy truth or faithfulness.

16.1:
children of the needy--

4. That peace, including prosperity, as an eminent characteristic of Christ's reign ( Is 2:4 ; Is 9:6 ; 11:9 ), will be illustrated in the security provided for the helpless and needy, and the punishment inflicted on oppressors, whose power to injure or mar the peace of others will be destroyed (compare Is 65:25 ; Zec 9:10 ). for the needy (compare sons of strangers, Psa 18:45 [Margin]).
16.7:
They that dwell in the wilderness--

9-11. The extent of the conquests. the wild, untutored tribes of deserts. in profound submission. The remotest and wealthiest nations shall acknowledge Him (compare Psa 45:12 ).
16.13: 12-14. They are not the conquests of arms, but the influences of humane and peaceful principles (compare Is 9:7 ; 11:1-9 ; Zec 9:9 , 10 ).

17.4: 18-19. These words close the Psalm in terms consistent with the style of the context, while Psa 72:20 is evidently, from its prosaic style, an addition for the purpose above explained [see on Psa 72:1].
17.10: 3-9. The prosperous wicked are insolently proud (compare Psa 5:5 ). They die, as well as live, free from perplexities: pride adorns them, and violence is their clothing; indeed they are inflated with unexpected success. With all this--

18.1: 3-9. The prosperous wicked are insolently proud (compare Psa 5:5 ). They die, as well as live, free from perplexities: pride adorns them, and violence is their clothing; indeed they are inflated with unexpected success. With all this--
18.7: 13-14. The Psalmist, partaking of these troubles, is especially disturbed in view of his own case, that with all his diligent efforts for a holy life, he is still sorely tried.

19.5:
18-20. their end--

future ( Psa 37:37 , 38 ), which is dismal and terribly sudden ( Pr 1:27 ; 29:1), aggravated and hastened by terror. As one despises an unsubstantial dream, so God, waking up to judgment ( Psa 7:6 ; 44:23 ), despises their vain shadow of happiness ( Psa 39:6 ; Is 29:7 ). They are thrown into ruins as a building falling to pieces ( Psa 74:3 ).
19.11: 23. Still he was with God, as a dependent beneficiary, and so kept from falling ( Psa 73:2 ).
19.17: 27-28. The lot of apostates, described by a figure of frequent use ( Jr 3:1 , 3 ; Eze 23:35 ), is contrasted with his, who finds happiness in nearness to God ( Jas 4:8 ), and his delightful work the declaration of His praise.
19.23:
4. roar--

with bestial fury. literally, "worshipping assemblies." literally, "signs"--substituted their idolatrous objects, or tokens of authority, for those articles of the temple which denoted God's presence.

20.3:
9. signs--

of God's presence, as altar, ark, &c. (compare Psa 74:4 ; 2Ch 36:18 , 19 ; Da 5:2 ). ( Is 3:2 ; Jr 40:1 ; 43:6 ). this is to last. Jeremiah's prophecy ( Jr 25:11 ), if published, may not have been generally known or understood. To the bulk of the people, during the captivity, the occasional and local prophetical services of Jeremiah, Ezekiel, and Daniel would not make an exception to the clause, "there is no more any prophet."

21.2:
divide the sea--

13-15. Examples of the "salvation wrought" are cited. that is, Red Sea. Pharaoh and his host (compare Is 51:9 , 10 ; Eze 29:3 , 4 ).
21.8:
19. multitude--

literally, "beast," their flock or company of men ( Psa 68:10 ). that is, the meek and lonely Church. literally, "the company," as above--thus the Church is represented as the spoiled and defeated remnant of an army, exposed to violence.

22.2:
Lift ... up the horn--

4-8. Here the writer speaks in view of God's declaration, warning the wicked. to exalt power, here, of the wicked himself--that is, to be arrogant or self-elated.
22.8: 9-10. Contrasted is the lot of the pious who will praise God, and, acting under His direction, will destroy the power of the wicked, and exalt that of the righteous.
22.14:
4. Thou--

God. great victorious nations, as Assyria ( Is 41:15 ; Eze 38:11 , 12 ; Zec 4:7 ).
22.20: 8-9. God's judgment on the wicked is His people's deliverance ( Psa 9:12 ; 10:7 ).

23.6:
was troubled--

3-9. His sad state contrasted with former joys. literally, "violently agitated," or disquieted ( Psa 39:6 ; 41:5 ). or, "fainted" ( Psa 107:5 ; Jon 2:7 ).
23.12: 11-12. He finds relief in contrasting God's former deliverances. Shall we receive good at His hands, and not evil? Both are orderings of unerring mercy and unfailing love.
23.18: 14-20. Illustrations of God's power in His special interventions for His people ( Ex 14:1-31 ), and, in the more common, but sublime, control of nature ( Psa 22:11-14 ; Ha 3:14 ) which may have attended those miraculous events ( Ex 14:24 ).

24.11: 3-8. This history had been handed down ( Ex 12:14 ; De 6:20 ) for God's honor, and that the principles of His law might be known and observed by posterity. This important sentiment is reiterated in ( Psa 78:7 , 8 ) negative form.
24.17: 9-11. The privileges of the first-born which belonged to Joseph ( 1Ch 5:1 , 2 ) were assigned to Ephraim by Jacob ( Ge 48:1 ). The supremacy of the tribe thus intimated was recognized by its position (in the marching of the nation to Canaan) next to the ark ( Nu 2:18-24 ), by the selection of the first permanent locality for the ark within its borders at Shiloh, and by the extensive and fertile province given for its possession. Traces of this prominence remained after the schism under Rehoboam, in the use, by later writers, of Ephraim for Israel (compare Ho 5:3-14; 11:3-12). Though a strong, well-armed tribe, and, from an early period, emulous and haughty (compare Jos 17:14 ; Jud 8:1-3 ; 2Sa 19:41 ), it appears, in this place, that it had rather led the rest in cowardice than courage; and had incurred God's displeasure, because, diffident of His promise, though often heretofore fulfilled, it had failed as a leader to carry out the terms of the covenant, by not driving out the heathen ( Ex 23:24 ; De 31:16 ; 2Ki 17:15 ).
24.23:
great depths--

15-16. There were two similar miracles ( Ex 17:6 ; Nu 20:11 ). and--rivers--denote abundance.

25.5:
17-20. yet more--

literally, "added to sin," instead of being led to repentance ( Ro 2:4 ).

26.4:
30-31. not estranged ... lust--

or, "desire"--that is, were indulging it.
26.10: 33-39. Though there were partial reformations after chastisement, and God, in pity, withdrew His hand for a time, yet their general conduct was rebellious, and He was thus provoked to waste and destroy them, by long and fruitless wandering in the desert.

27.6:
50-51. made a way--

removed obstacles, gave it full scope.
27.12:
55. by line--

or, the portion thus measured. that is, the heathen, put for their possessions, so tents--that is, of the heathen (compare De 6:11 ).

28.4:
59-60. heard--

perceived ( Ge 11:7 ). but not utterly.
28.16:
70-72. God's sovereignty was illustrated in this choice. The contrast is striking--

humility and exaltation--and the correspondence is beautiful.
28.28:
called upon--

6-7. (Compare Jr 10:25 ). Though we deserve much, do not the heathen deserve more for their violence to us ( Jr 51:3-5 ; Zec 1:14 )? The singular denotes the chief power, and the use of the plural indicates the combined confederates. or, "by" proclaimed Thy attributes and professed allegiance ( Is 12:4 ; Ac 2:21 ).

29.4:
12. into their bosom--

The lap or folds of the dress is used by Eastern people for receiving articles. The figure denotes retaliation (compare Is 65:6 , 7 ). They reproached God as well as His people.
29.10:
3. Turn us--

that is, from captivity. ( Nu 6:25 ).
29.16:
8-11. brought--

or, "plucked up," as by roots, to be replanted. ( Psa 78:47 ). The figure ( Is 16:8 ) represents the flourishing state of Israel, as predicted ( Ge 28:14 ), and verified ( 1Ki 4:20-25 ).
29.22:
13. The boar--

may represent the ravaging Assyrian and other heathen.

30.3: 18. We need quickening grace ( Psa 71:20 ; 119:25 ) to persevere in Thy right worship ( Ge 4:26 ; Ro 10:11 ).
30.9:
3. the new moon--

or the month. (Compare Pr 7:20 ).
30.15:
if thou wilt hearken--

8. (Compare Psa 50:7 ). The reproof follows to Psa 81:12 . He then propounds the terms of His covenant: they should worship Him alone, who ( Psa 81:10 ) had delivered them, and would still confer all needed blessings.
30.21: 13-16. Obedience would have secured all promised blessings and the subjection of foes. In this passage, "should have," "would have," &c., are better, "should" and "would" expressing God's intention at the time, that is, when they left Egypt.
30.27:
1. congregation--

(Compare Ex 12:3 ; 16:1 ). that is, of God, of His appointment. or, "judges" ( Ex 21:6 ; 22:9 ), God's representatives.
30.33: 6-7. Though God admitted their official dignity ( Joh 10:34 ), He reminds them of their mortality.

31.5:
2. thine enemies--

as well as ours ( Psa 74:23 ; Is 37:23 ).

32.1:
6-8. tabernacles--

for people ( Psa 78:67 ). all these united with the children of Lot, or Ammonites and Moabites (compare 2Ch 20:1 ).
32.7:
houses--

12. The language of the invaders. literally, "residences," enclosures, as for flocks ( Psa 65:12 ). as the proprietors of the land ( 2Ch 20:11 ; Is 14:25 ).
32.19:
3. thine altars--

that is, of burnt offering and incense, used for the whole tabernacle. Its structure afforded facilities for sparrows and swallows to indulge their known predilections for such places. Some understand the statement as to the birds as a comparison: "as they find homes, so do I desire thine altars," &c.

33.16: 4-7. having still occasion for the anger which is deprecated.
33.22: 10. God's promises of "mercy" will be verified by His "truth" (compare Psa 25:10 ; 40:10 ); and the "work of righteousness" in His holy government shall be "peace" ( Is 32:17 ). There is an implied contrast with a dispensation under which God's truth sustains His threatened wrath, and His righteousness inflicts misery on the wicked.

34.3:
1-2. poor and needy--

a suffering child of God, as in Psa 10:12 , 17 ; 18:27 . or, "godly," as in Psa 4:3 ; 85:8 .
34.9:
5-7. unto all ... that call upon thee--

or, "worship Thee" ( Psa 50:15 ; 91:15 ) however undeserving ( Ex 34:6 ; Le 11:9-13 ).
34.15:
11. Teach--

Show, point out. of Providence. according to its declarations. fix all my affections ( Psa 12:2 ; Jas 4:8 ). (compare Psa 86:12 ) to honor Thy perfections.

35.9:
3. spoken of thee--

or, "in thee," that is, the city of God ( Psa 46:4 ; 48:2 ).

36.10:
5. Free ... dead--

Cut off from God's care, as are the slain, who, falling under His wrath, are left, no longer sustained by His hand.
36.16:
10. shall the dead--

the remains of ghosts. literally, "rise up," that is, as dead persons.
36.22:
15. from ... youth up--

all my life.

37.5:
1. mercies--

those promised ( Is 55:3 ; Ac 13:34 ), and-- that is, in fulfilling them.
37.11:
sons of the mighty--

6-7. This is worthy of our belief, for His faithfulness (is praised) by the congregation of saints or holy ones; that is, angels (compare De 33:2 ; Da 8:13 ). (compare Psa 29:1 ). So is He to be admired on earth.
37.17: 8-14. To illustrate His power and faithfulness examples are cited from history. His control of the sea (the most mighty and unstable object in nature), and of Egypt ( Psa 87:4 ), the first great foe of Israel (subjected to utter helplessness from pride and insolence), are specimens. At the same time, the whole frame of nature founded and sustained by Him, Tabor and Hermon for "east and west," and "north and south," together representing the whole world, declare the same truth as to His attributes.
37.23:
16-17. in--

or, "by" Thy faithful just rule. or, "beauty." They shall be adorned as well as protected. exalt our power ( Psa 75:10 ; Lu 1:69 ).
37.29:
19-37. Then--

when the covenant was established, of whose execution the exalted views of God now given furnish assurance. or godly saint, object of favor ( Psa 4:3 ). Nathan is meant ( 2Sa 7:17 ; 1Ch 17:3-15 ). literally, "given help." David was chosen and then exalted.
37.35:
19-37. Then--

when the covenant was established, of whose execution the exalted views of God now given furnish assurance. or godly saint, object of favor ( Psa 4:3 ). Nathan is meant ( 2Sa 7:17 ; 1Ch 17:3-15 ). literally, "given help." David was chosen and then exalted.

38.2:
19-37. Then--

when the covenant was established, of whose execution the exalted views of God now given furnish assurance. or godly saint, object of favor ( Psa 4:3 ). Nathan is meant ( 2Sa 7:17 ; 1Ch 17:3-15 ). literally, "given help." David was chosen and then exalted.
38.8:
19-37. Then--

when the covenant was established, of whose execution the exalted views of God now given furnish assurance. or godly saint, object of favor ( Psa 4:3 ). Nathan is meant ( 2Sa 7:17 ; 1Ch 17:3-15 ). literally, "given help." David was chosen and then exalted.
38.14:
cast off--

38-52. present a striking contrast to these glowing promises, in mournful evidences of a loss of God's favor. and rejected (compare Psa 15:4 ; 43:2 ; 44:9 ).
38.20:
cast off--

38-52. present a striking contrast to these glowing promises, in mournful evidences of a loss of God's favor. and rejected (compare Psa 15:4 ; 43:2 ; 44:9 ).

39.3:
cast off--

38-52. present a striking contrast to these glowing promises, in mournful evidences of a loss of God's favor. and rejected (compare Psa 15:4 ; 43:2 ; 44:9 ).

40.6:
7-8. For--

A reason, this is the infliction of God's wrath. literally, "confounded by terror" ( Psa 2:5 ). Death is by sin ( Ro 5:12 ). Though "secret," the light of God's countenance, as a candle, will bring sin to view ( Pr 20:27 ; 1Co 4:5 ).
40.12:
let it repent--

13. (Compare Psa 13:2 ). a strong figure, as in Ex 32:12 , imploring a change in His dealings.
40.24:
5. terror--

or, what causes it ( Pr 20:2 ). then aggravated. that is, of enemies.
40.30: 9-12. This exemption from evil is the result of trust in God, who employs angels as ministering spirits ( He 1:14 ).

41.4:
set his love--

14-16. God Himself speaks (compare Psa 46:10 ; 75:2 , 3 ). All the terms to express safety and peace indicate the most undoubting confidence (compare Psa 18:2 ; 20:1 ; 22:5 ). that of the most ardent kind.
41.10:
with a ... sound--

3. In such a work all proper aid must be used. or, on Higgaion (see on Psa 9:16 ), perhaps an instrument of that name, from its sound resembling the muttered sound of meditation, as expressed also by the word. This is joined with the harp.
41.16:
most high--

8. This he does in part, by contrasting their ruin with God's exaltation and eternity. as occupying the highest place in heaven ( Psa 7:7 ; 18:16 ).
41.22: 12-14. The vigorous growth, longevity, utility, fragrance, and beauty of these noble trees, set forth the life, character, and destiny of the pious;
41.28: 2-4. His underived power exceeds the most sublime exhibitions of the most powerful objects in nature ( Psa 89:9 ).

42.4:
show thyself--

1-2. God's revenge is His judicial infliction of righteous punishment. (Compare Margin).
42.10:
5-6. thy people [and] thine heritage--

are synonymous, the people being often called God's heritage. As justice to the weak is a sign of the best government, their oppression is a sign of the worst ( De 10:18 ; Is 10:2 ).
42.16: 9-11. The evidence of God's providential government is found in His creative power and omniscience, which also assure us that He can punish the wicked in regard to all their vain purposes.
42.22: 16. These questions imply that none other than God will help ( Psa 60:9 ),

43.8:
2. come ... presence--

literally, "approach," or, meet Him ( Psa 17:13 ).
43.14: 7. This relation illustrates our entire dependence (compare Psa 23:3 ; 74:1 ). The last clause is united by Paul ( He 3:7 ) to the following (compare Psa 81:8 ),
43.26: 4-5. For He is not a local God, but of universal agency, while idols are nothing.

44.3: 10. Let all know that the government of the world is ordered in justice, and they shall enjoy firm and lasting peace (compare Psa 72:3 , 7 ; Is 9:6 , 7 ).
44.9: 1-2. This dominion is a cause of joy, because, even though our minds are oppressed with terror before the throne of the King of kings ( Ex 19:16 ; De 5:22 ), we know it is based on righteous principles and judgments which are according to truth.
44.15:
6. heavens--

or, their inhabitants ( Psa 50:6 ), as opposed to "nations" in the latter clause (compare Is 40:5 ; 66:18 ).
44.21: 10-12. Let gratitude for the blessings of providence and grace incite saints ( Psa 4:3 ) to holy living. Spiritual blessings are in store, represented by light ( Psa 27:1 ) and gladness.
44.27: 3. The union of mercy and truth ( Psa 57:3 ; 85:10 ) secure the blessings of the promise ( Ge 12:3 ; 18:18 ) to all the world ( Is 52:10 ).

45.10:
3. thy ... name--

perfections of justice, power, &c. producing dread ( De 10:17 ), and to be praised by those over whom He is exalted ( Psa 97:9 ). or, "He is holy" ( Psa 99:5 , 9 ; Is 6:3 ).
45.16:
among ... priests, among ... upon the Lord [and] He spake ... pillar--

6-8. The experience of these servants of God is cited for encouragement. may be referred to all three (compare Ex 18:19 ; Le 8:15 ; De 5:5 ; 1Sa 9:13 ).
45.22: 3. To the obligations of a creature and subject is added that of a beneficiary ( Psa 95:7 ).

46.2: 2. He avows his sincere purpose, by God's aid, to act uprightly ( Ge 17:1 ; Psa 18:30 ).
46.8:
7. not dwell--

literally, "not sit," or tarry, or be established.

47.6:
8. sworn against me--

or literally, "by me," wishing others as miserable as I am ( Nu 5:21 ).
47.12:
for--

13-14. Hence it is here adduced. or, "when." the time promised, the indication of which is the interest felt for Zion by the people of God.

48.2:
18. people ... created--

(compare Psa 22:31 ), an organized body, as a Church.
48.8:
in the way--

23-28. The writer, speaking for the Church, finds encouragement in the midst of all his distresses. God's eternal existence is a pledge of faithfulness to His promises. of providence. literally, "afflicted," and made fearful of a premature end, a figure of the apprehensions of the Church, lest God might not perform His promise, drawn from those of a person in view of the dangers of early death (compare Psa 89:47 ). Paul ( He 1:10 ) quotes Psa 102:26-28 as addressed to Christ in His divine nature. The scope of the Psalm, as already seen, so far from opposing, favors this view, especially by the sentiments of Psa 102:12-15 (compare Is 60:1 ). The association of the Messiah with a day of future glory to the Church was very intimate in the minds of Old Testament writers; and with correct views of His nature it is very consistent that He should be addressed as the Lord and Head of His Church, who would bring about that glorious future on which they ever dwelt with fond delightful anticipations.
48.14:
in the way--

23-28. The writer, speaking for the Church, finds encouragement in the midst of all his distresses. God's eternal existence is a pledge of faithfulness to His promises. of providence. literally, "afflicted," and made fearful of a premature end, a figure of the apprehensions of the Church, lest God might not perform His promise, drawn from those of a person in view of the dangers of early death (compare Psa 89:47 ). Paul ( He 1:10 ) quotes Psa 102:26-28 as addressed to Christ in His divine nature. The scope of the Psalm, as already seen, so far from opposing, favors this view, especially by the sentiments of Psa 102:12-15 (compare Is 60:1 ). The association of the Messiah with a day of future glory to the Church was very intimate in the minds of Old Testament writers; and with correct views of His nature it is very consistent that He should be addressed as the Lord and Head of His Church, who would bring about that glorious future on which they ever dwelt with fond delightful anticipations.
48.20:
4. redeemeth--

Cost is implied. literally, "pit of corruption" ( Psa 16:10 ). or, "adorneth" ( Psa 65:11 ). compassions (compare Psa 25:6 ; 40:11 ).

49.3:
keep (anger)--

8-10. God's benevolence implies no merit. He shows it to sinners, who also are chastened for a time ( Ex 34:6 ). in Le 19:18 , bear a grudge ( Jr 3:5 , 12 ).
49.9:
14. he--

"who formed," Psa 94:9 . literally, "our form." made of and tending to it ( Ge 2:7 ).
49.15: 19. God's firm and universal dominion is a pledge that He will keep His promises ( Psa 11:4 ; 47:8 ).
49.21: 1. God's essential glory, and also that displayed by His mighty works, afford ground for praise.

50.6: 10-13. Once destructive, these waters are subjected to the service of God's creatures. In rain and dew from His chambers (compare Psa 104:3 ), and fountains and streams, they give drink to thirsting animals and fertilize the soil. Trees thus nourished supply homes to singing birds, and the earth teems with the productions of God's wise agencies,

51.6: 20-23. He provides and adapts to man's wants the appointed times and seasons.
51.12: 24-26. From a view of the earth thus full of God's blessings, the writer passes to the sea, which, in its immensity, and as a scene and means of man's activity in commerce, and the home of countless multitudes of creatures, also displays divine power and beneficence. The mention of
51.18: 31-34. While God could equally glorify His power in destruction, that He does it in preservation is of His rich goodness and mercy, so that we may well spend our lives in grateful praise, honoring to Him, and delightful to pious hearts ( Psa 147:1 ).

52.6:
5-6. judgments ... mouth--

His judicial decisions for the good and against the wicked.
52.12:
word--

8-11. The covenant was often ratified. answering to "covenant" [Psa 105:9] in the parallel clause, namely, the word of promise, which, according to Psa 105:10 , He set forth for an inviolable law. or, "ordained" ( Psa 68:28 ). perpetually. A verbal allusion to De 7:9 (compare Ex 20:6 ).

53.2:
12-15. few ... in number--

alluding to Jacob's words ( Ge 34:30 ), "I being few in number." literally, "as a few," that is, like fewness itself (compare Is 1:9 ). sojourners in the land of their future inheritance, as in a strange country ( He 11:9 ).
53.8: 17-21. Joseph was sent of God ( Ge 45:5 ).

54.1:
23-25. Israel ... and Jacob--

that is, Jacob himself is meant, as Psa 105:24 speaks of "his people." Still, he came with his whole house ( Ge 46:6 , 7 ). ( Ge 47:4 ). or, Egypt ( Psa 78:51 ).
54.7:
they rebelled not--

28-36. The ninth plague is made prominent as peculiarly wonderful. Moses and Aaron promptly obeyed God ( He 11:27 ); (compare Ex 7: 1-11:10 and Psa 78:44-51 , with which this summary substantially agrees). Or, rather, the "darkness" here is figurative ( Jr 13:16 ), the literal plague of darkness ( Ex 10:22 , 23 ) being only alluded to as the symbol of God's wrath which overhung Egypt as a dark cloud during all the plagues. Hence, it is placed first, out of the historical order. Thus, "They rebelled not (that is, no longer) against His word," refers to the Egyptians. Whenever God sent a plague on them, they were ready to let Israel go, though refusing when the plague ceased. His command to let Israel go [Hengstenberg]. Of the ten plagues, only eight are mentioned, the fifth, the murrain of beasts, and the sixth, the boils, being omitted.
54.13:
they rebelled not--

28-36. The ninth plague is made prominent as peculiarly wonderful. Moses and Aaron promptly obeyed God ( He 11:27 ); (compare Ex 7: 1-11:10 and Psa 78:44-51 , with which this summary substantially agrees). Or, rather, the "darkness" here is figurative ( Jr 13:16 ), the literal plague of darkness ( Ex 10:22 , 23 ) being only alluded to as the symbol of God's wrath which overhung Egypt as a dark cloud during all the plagues. Hence, it is placed first, out of the historical order. Thus, "They rebelled not (that is, no longer) against His word," refers to the Egyptians. Whenever God sent a plague on them, they were ready to let Israel go, though refusing when the plague ceased. His command to let Israel go [Hengstenberg]. Of the ten plagues, only eight are mentioned, the fifth, the murrain of beasts, and the sixth, the boils, being omitted.

55.7:
remembered ... and Abraham--

42-45. The reasons for these dealings: (1) God's faithfulness to His covenant, "His holy promise" of Canaan, is the fountain whence flowed so many acts of marvellous kindness to His people (compare Psa 105:8 , 11 ). Ex 2:24 is the fundamental passage [Hengstenberg]. (2) That they might be obedient. The observance of God's commands by Abraham was the object of the covenant with him ( Ge 18:19 ), as it was also the object of the covenant with Israel, that they might observe God's statutes. or, "remembered His holy word (that is, covenant confirmed) with Abraham."
55.13:
visit--

4-5. In view of the desert of sins to be confessed, the writer invokes God's covenant mercy to himself and the Church, in whose welfare he rejoices. The speaker, me, I, is not the Psalmist himself, but the people, the present generation (compare Psa 106:6 ). (Compare Psa 8:4 ).

56.5:
the sea ... the Red Sea--

7-12. Special confession. Their rebellion at the sea ( Ex 14:11 ) was because they had not remembered nor understood God's miracles on their behalf. That God saved them in their unbelief was of His mere mercy, and for His own glory. the very words in which Moses' song celebrated the scene of Israel's deliverance ( Ex 15:4 ). Israel began to rebel against God at the very moment and scene of its deliverance by God!
56.11:
They soon forgat--

13-15. The faith induced by God's display of power in their behalf was short lived, and their new rebellion and temptation was visited by God with fresh punishment, inflicted by leaving them to the result of their own gratified appetites, and sending on them spiritual poverty ( Nu 11:18 ). literally, "They hasted, they forgat" (compare Ex 32:8 ). "They have turned aside quickly (or, hastily) out of the way." The haste of our desires is such that we can scarcely allow God one day. Unless He immediately answers our call, instantly then arise impatience, and at length despair. ( De 11:3 , 4 ; Da 9:14 ). They waited not for the development of God's counsel, or plan for their deliverance, at His own time, and in His own way.

57.4:
made--

19-23. From indirect setting God at naught, they pass to direct. though prohibited in Ex 20:4 , 5 to make a likeness, even of the true God. called so in contempt. They would have made an ox or bull, but their idol turned out but a calf; an imitation of the divine symbols, the cherubim; or of the sacred bull of Egyptian idolatry. The idolatry was more sinful in view of their recent experience of God's power in Egypt and His wonders at Sinai ( Ex 32:1-6 ). Though intending to worship Jehovah under the symbol of the calf, yet as this was incompatible with His nature ( De 4:15-17 ), they in reality gave up Him, and so were given up by Him. Instead of the Lord of heaven, they had as their glory the image of an ox that does nothing but eat grass.
57.10:
despised--

24-27. The sin of refusing to invade Canaan, "the pleasant land" ( Jr 3:19 ; Eze 20:6 ; Da 8:9 ), "the land of beauty," was punished by the destruction of that generation ( Nu 14:28 ), and the threat of dispersion ( De 4:25 ; 28:32 ) afterwards made to their posterity, and fulfilled in the great calamities now bewailed, may have also been then added. ( Nu 14:31 ). by which He promised He would give them the land; but rather the word of the faithless spies (compare Psa 78:22 ).
57.16:
28-30. sacrifices of the dead--

that is, of lifeless idols, contrasted with "the living God" ( Jr 10:3-10 ; compare Psa 115:4-7 ; 1Co 12:2 ). On the words, see Nu 25:2 , 3, 5 . that is, the possessor of Peor, the mountain on which Chemosh, the idol of Moab, was worshipped, and at the foot of which Israel at the time lay encamped ( Nu 23:28 ). The name never occurs except in connection with that locality and that circumstance.

58.6:
commanded--

34-39. They not only failed to expel the heathen, as God ( Ex 23:32 , 33 ), literally, "said (they should)," but conformed to their idolatries [Psa 106:36], and thus became spiritual adulterers ( Psa 73:27 ).
58.12: 44-46. If, as is probable, this Psalm was written at the time of the captivity, the writer now intimates the tokens of God's returning favor.

59.9:
city of habitation--

4-7. A graphic picture is given of the sufferings of those who from distant lands returned to Jerusalem; or, may mean the land of Palestine.
59.15:
10-16. Their sufferings were for their rebellion against ( Psa 105:28 ) the words, or purposes, or promises, of God for their benefit. When humbled they cry to God, who delivers them from bondage, described as a dark dungeon with doors and bars of metal, in which they are bound in iron--

that is, chains and fetters. darkness with danger ( Psa 23:4 ).
59.21:
10-16. Their sufferings were for their rebellion against ( Psa 105:28 ) the words, or purposes, or promises, of God for their benefit. When humbled they cry to God, who delivers them from bondage, described as a dark dungeon with doors and bars of metal, in which they are bound in iron--

that is, chains and fetters. darkness with danger ( Psa 23:4 ).

60.5:
are afflicted--

17-22. Whether the same or not, this exigency illustrates that dispensation of God according to which sin brings its own punishment. literally, "afflict themselves," that is, bring on disease, denoted by loathing of food, and drawing
60.11:
go ... sea--

23-32. Here are set forth the perils of seafaring, futility of man's, and efficiency of God's, help. alluding to the elevation of the land at the coast.
60.17:
go ... sea--

23-32. Here are set forth the perils of seafaring, futility of man's, and efficiency of God's, help. alluding to the elevation of the land at the coast.

61.6:
33-41. He turneth rivers into a wilderness, &c.--

God's providence is illustriously displayed in His influence on two great elements of human prosperity, the earth's productiveness and the powers of government. He punishes the wicked by destroying the sources of fertility, or, in mercy, gives fruitfulness to deserts, which become the homes of a busy and successful agricultural population. By a permitted misrule and tyranny, this scene of prosperity is changed to one of adversity. He rules rulers, setting up one and putting down another.

63.5:
2. For the mouth ... opened--

or, "They have opened a wicked mouth" literally, "with me," that is, Their intercourse is lying, or, they slander me to my face ( Mt 26:59 ).
63.11: 7. The condemnation is aggravated when prayer for relief is treated as a sin.

64.3: 17-19. Let his loved sin, cursing, come upon him in punishment ( Psa 35:8 ), thoroughly fill him as water and oil, permeating to every part of his system (compare Nu 5:22-27 ), and become a garment and a girdle for a perpetual dress.
64.9:
21-22. do ... for me--

that is, kindness. literally, "pierced" ( Psa 69:16 , 29 ).

65.2: 26-27. Let my deliverance glorify Thee (compare Psa 59:13 ).
65.14:
5. at thy right hand--

as Psa 109:31 , upholding and aiding, which is not inconsistent with Psa 110:1 , where the figure denotes participation of power, for here He is presented in another aspect, as a warrior going against enemies, and sustained by God. smite or crush. not common men, but their rulers, and so all under them ( Psa 2:2 , 10 ).
65.20:
sought--

2. His works, that is, of providence and grace are or, carefully studied, by all desiring to know them.

66.6: 1. True fear produces obedience and this happiness.
66.12: 5-9. Generosity, sound judgment in business, and confidence in God, form a character which preserves from fear of evil and ensures success against enemies. While a man thus truly pious is liberal, he increases in substance.
